original_abstract: The electrochemical synthesis of ammonia via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) offers a sustainable alternative to the Haber-Bosch process, yet its industrial implementation is hindered by inefficiency in concentrated electrolytes. For many high-performance catalysts, such as Cu2O, a high nitrate concentration leads to surface poisoning, where nitrate adsorbents obstruct the hydrogen acquisition sites necessary for subsequent hydrogenation steps. In this work, we address this kinetic mismatch by engineering ruthenium clusters onto Cu2O nanowires (Ru-Cu2O). We demonstrate that the ruthenium domains function as catalytic "hot spots" that facilitate hydrogen adsorption and its subsequent transfer to the Cu2O matrix, thereby maintaining efficient reaction kinetics even in nitrate-rich environments. Under 1 M nitrate conditions, the Ru-Cu2O catalyst maintains a ~100% Faradaic efficiency toward ammonia. When integrated into a 16 cm² flow electrolyzer, the system achieves a remarkable full-cell current of 10.0 A, producing ammonia at an estimated cost of 6.87 USD per kilogram. Mechanistic investigations using deuterium labeling, operando Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and ab-initio molecular dynamics simulations reveal that ruthenium lowers the energy barriers for proton-coupled electron transfer steps. This study provides a strategy for overcoming kinetic bottlenecks in electrosynthesis by optimizing synergistic interactions in co-catalytic systems across a full range of electrolyte concentrations.
humanized_abstract: The nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) for the electrochemical synthesis of ammonia using ammonia synthesis using the Haber-Bosch process is attractive but the process is not very efficient in concentrated electrolytes. While many high performance catalysts, like Cu2O, can yield high activity and ammonia selectivity in diluted solutions, a high nitrate concentration leads to surface poisoning caused by adsorption of nitrate species on the catalyst surface that block the hydrogen acquisition sites required for further hydrogenation. To alleviate this kinetically mismatched effect, we prepared ruthenium clusters on Cu2O nanowires (Ru-Cu2O). Ru-domains act as catalytic hot spots that facilitate the hydrogen adsorption and ammonia synthesis with sufficient reaction kinetics in nitrate concentrated electrolyte solutions. Under 1 M nitrate solution, Ru-Cu2O catalysts can retain ~100% Faradaic efficiency towards ammonia production. An ammonia flow electrolyzer using Ru-Cu2O as catalyst achieves a full-cell current of 10.0 A at 16 cm² electrodes and corresponding ammonia price is 6.87 USD/kg. Using a combination of deuterium labelling experiments, operando Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and ab-initio molecular dynamics simulations, the positive effect of Ru on the Cu2O catalyst was demonstrated by identifying the proton-coupled electron transfer process and its corresponding energy barriers that are significantly decreased in the Ru-Cu2O catalysts. Overall, the present work exemplifies a new concept to optimize catalytic performance in the process of co-catalysts in various solutions from dilute to concentrated.
